<Input Text>
```
Document ID: DOC-004
Title: Supply Chain & Allocation Policy APAC
Category: Supply Chain
Content: APAC Supply Chain Management — Inventory & Boutique Allocation Policy

NETWORK OVERVIEW
Aurelle APAC operates a dual-hub supply chain model designed to balance speed-to-market, customs efficiency, and boutique inventory health across 13 markets and 67 boutique/sales points.

Regional Hubs:
- Singapore Distribution Centre (SDC): Primary hub for Southeast Asia (SG, TH, PH, MY, IN, AU). Total warehousing capacity: 12,000 units. Average transit to boutique: 3–5 business days. Customs clearance handled centrally for ASEAN markets.
- Switzerland Direct (ZHD): North Asia (Mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan) supplied directly from Manufacture in Le Brassus/Geneva via Swiss Air Freight. Reason: Lower transit time for high-value items, dedicated customs broker relationships, insurance coverage preferred for pieces >CHF 100,000.
- India Supply Corridor: India is supplied from SDC with a separate customs clearance broker in Mumbai due to BIS hallmarking requirements for gold jewellery. Lead time: 7–10 business days.

PRODUCT CATEGORIES AND SUPPLY APPROACH
- Watches (perennial references — Tank Must, Santos, Ballon Bleu): Forecast-driven replenishment on 8-week rolling cycle. Demand signals from boutique POS integrated with SAP S/4HANA allocation module.
- Watches (limited and complications): Allocated annually by HQ in Geneva based on market quotas. APAC quota negotiated by Regional Supply Chain VP with HQ in November for following year.
- Classic Jewellery (LOVE, Trinity, Juste un Clou perennial): Replenishment-driven, 6-week cycle. Fastest-moving SKUs (LOVE bracelet YG, Trinity ring classic) have dedicated safety stock at SDC of minimum 45 units.
- High Jewellery: Piece-by-piece allocation. Each piece has a unique piece number registered in the Aurelle master inventory system. Cannot be duplicated or substituted. Movement tracked at piece level in real time.
- Fragrances & Accessories: Managed separately through commercial distribution network; not covered under jewellery/watches allocation policy.

ALLOCATION OPTIMIZER PROTOCOL
For limited stock of high-demand references (defined as any reference with waitlist >10 names APAC-wide), the Allocation Optimizer engine applies the following weighted criteria:
1. CRM Waitlist Backlog (35% weight): Stores with confirmed VIC clients awaiting specific pieces receive priority.
2. Sales Velocity (25% weight): 90-day rolling average of units sold per available display day.
3. Boutique Tier (20% weight): Tier 1 Maison flagship > Tier 2 flagships > Standard boutiques.
4. Stock Cover Weeks (20% weight): Stores with <3 weeks cover on a reference trigger urgent allocation flag.
The engine runs weekly (every Monday) and outputs an allocation recommendation reviewed by Regional Supply Chain Manager before execution. Market Directors may appeal allocation decisions within 48 hours with documented VIC justification.

EMERGENCY STOCK PROTOCOL
Boutique Directors may file an Emergency Stock Request (ESR) directly to Switzerland (copy Regional Supply Chain) when:
- A confirmed VIC (Tier 1) has expressed intent to purchase a specific reference not currently in boutique stock
- A High Jewellery event is confirmed with specific piece requirements within 14 days
ESR commitments: Delivery within 5 business days for watches/classic jewellery, 7 business days for High Jewellery (additional security packaging and insurance required). ESR is tracked in CRM as a client commitment — failure to deliver on ESR timeline requires Market Director escalation and client communication within 24 hours of identified delay.

INVENTORY HEALTH TARGETS (FY2025)
- Weeks of Cover target: 10–14 weeks for standard references, 6–8 weeks for limited
- Sell-Through Rate target: >72% per collection within 12 months
- Dead Stock threshold: Any unit unsold for >18 months is reviewed for inter-boutique transfer or repatriation to SDC
- Shrinkage target: <0.05% of total inventory value (luxury-grade security protocol mandatory)

INTER-BOUTIQUE TRANSFERS
Boutique Directors may request transfers from neighboring boutiques within the same market (e.g., Shanghai Plaza 66 → Shanghai Jing An) without Regional Supply Chain approval for pieces valued below USD 30,000. Cross-market transfers (e.g., HK → Singapore) require Regional Supply Chain approval and customs documentation. High Jewellery pieces: any transfer requires security escort and HQ notification regardless of value.
```

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**

```
while Alex clenched his jaw, the buzz of frustration dull against the backdrop of Taylor's authoritarian certainty. It was this competitive undercurrent that kept him alert, the sense that his and Jordan's shared commitment to discovery was an unspoken rebellion against Cruz's narrowing vision of control and order.

Then Taylor did something unexpected. They paused beside Jordan and, for a moment, observed the device with something akin to reverence. "If this tech can be understood..." Taylor said, their voice quieter, "It could change the game for us. For all of us."

The underlying dismissal earlier seemed to falter, replaced by a glimpse of reluctant respect for the gravity of what lay in their hands. Jordan looked up, and for a fleeting heartbeat, their eyes locked with Taylor's, a wordless clash of wills softening into an uneasy truce.

It was a small transformation, barely perceptible, but one that Alex noted with an inward nod. They had all been brought here by different paths
```

    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.

```
Document ID: DOC-016
Title: After-Sales Service & Client Care Protocol APAC
Category: Operations
Content: Aurelle After-Sales Service & Client Care — APAC Protocol

SERVICE AS A RELATIONSHIP TOOL
After-sales service in the luxury context is not a cost center — it is one of the most powerful relationship touchpoints available. A client who brings their 10-year-old LOVE bracelet in for polishing and leaves with it gleaming and a personal note from their stylist is a client who will make their next significant purchase at Aurelle. The service interaction must be treated with the same intentionality as a new sale.

SERVICE CENTER NETWORK APAC
- Singapore Service Centre (SSC): Primary regional service hub for Southeast Asia, India, Australia. Workshop capacity: 45 watchmakers, 12 jewellery craftspeople. TAT (turnaround time) target: 3 weeks standard, 5 weeks for complex complications.
- Tokyo Service Centre: Dedicated to Japan market. Highest capacity per market — 28 watchmakers, 8 jewellery craftspeople. TAT: 2 weeks standard (Japan client expectations are most stringent for service timelines).
- Shanghai Service Centre: Mainland China and Taiwan. Regulatory requirement: all repairs on China-duty-paid items must be serviced domestically or documented through official customs channels for border repair. TAT: 3–4 weeks.
- Seoul Workshop: South Korea, recently upgraded. 18 watchmakers, 6 jewellery craftspeople. TAT: 2.5 weeks standard.
- Hong Kong Workshop: HK and Macau. Also handles high-complexity HJ restoration. TAT: 3 weeks.
- Certificate of Service: Issued for all watch services — records movement condition, parts replaced, water resistance test result, power reserve test. Adds to the piece's provenance and value.

WATCH SERVICE STANDARDS
Recommended full service intervals (communicated to clients at point of sale):
- Quartz movements: 3–5 years (battery, seal, timing check)
- Automatic movements: 5–7 years (full movement service, lubrication, gasket replacement)
- High complications: 7–10 years (manufacturer recommends service at Aurelle only)

Client communication on service: Stylist proactively initiates service reminder based on purchase date recorded in CRM. Do not wait for client to complain of malfunction — preventive outreach builds trust.

Warranty:
- International warranty: 2 years from purchase on all watches (movement and parts, excludes physical damage, water damage beyond rated depth, battery)
- Extended service plan: Aurelle Prestige Care (optional, sold at point of purchase): 5-year extension, includes 1 complimentary full service and priority TAT.
- For Tier 1 clients: complimentary watch service as per segmentation policy (DOC-003
```

---Role---
You are a Knowledge Graph Specialist responsible for extracting entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.
        *   `entity_description`: Provide a concise yet comprehensive description of the entity's attributes and activities, based *solely* on the information present in the input text.
    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

```
Document ID: DOC-010
Title: Market Intelligence — Southeast Asia & India
Category: Market Intelligence
Content: Southeast Asia & India Market Intelligence — APAC Coverage

SINGAPORE

Market Role: Singapore is simultaneously an Aurelle market and the APAC regional headquarters city. Revenue target FY2025: USD 38M. Singapore's luxury market punches far above its population weight due to: (1) Very high per-capita income and wealth concentration, (2) HNWI immigration (Global Investor Programme and family office ecosystem), (3) Tourism-driven spend (Changi hub — luxury tourists pause for boutique visits), (4) Corporate gifting market driven by MNCs headquartered in Singapore.

Boutique Network:
- ION Orchard Maison (flagship): USD 16M — primary VIC engagement venue
- Marina Bay Sands: USD 10M — tourist-dominant, strong watches, Japanese and Chinese tourist high-spend
- Takashimaya Ngee Ann City: USD 7M — domestic client base, mix of resident and expatriate
- Jewel Changi: USD 5M — transit luxury, smaller format

Consumer Profile:
- Multi-cultural complexity: Chinese Singaporean (60%), Malay (15%), Indian (9%), expatriate (significant): each sub-segment has distinct luxury behavior. Chinese Singaporean: LOVE and watches, gifting culture; Indian Singaporean: gold-centric, gravitating toward bridal and Trinity; expatriate (Western): watches and fragrance dominant.
- New wealth immigrants: Ultra-high-net-worth families relocating from China, Indonesia, and India — CRM onboarding of newly arrived HNW residents is a priority initiative (collaboration with private banking partners and family office networks).
- Price sensitivity relative to market: Singapore pricing is relatively competitive versus other APAC markets — clients are informed and may price-compare. Focus on relationship and exclusivity value.

THAILAND

Revenue target FY2025: USD 18M. Bangkok is the key city with two boutiques: ICONSIAM Maison (flagship, USD 11M) and Siam Paragon (USD 7M). Thai luxury market is driven by: local HNWI (industrialist and banking families), Chinese-Thai business elite, and tourism (Phuket, Koh Samui — seasonal boutique consideration for future). Thai clients have a very strong social and cultural relationship with jewelry as personal adornment and status symbol — this aligns naturally with Aurelle's jewellery positioning. Buddhist calendar key dates (Songkran, Makha Bucha) drive gifting. Chinese New Year also significant for Chinese-Thai community. Royal family association with European luxury brands is a cultural reference point — historical brand connections are valued.

PHILIPPINES

Revenue target FY2025: USD 14M. Manila boutiques: Greenbelt 5 Makati (USD 8M), BGC High Street (USD 6M). The Philippines has an extremely concentrated luxury consumer base — an estimated 80% of luxury purchases are made by approximately 200 prominent families whose social networks are tight and interlocked. Word-of-mouth within this community is the single most powerful CRM tool. Event-based selling (private dinners at Ayala mansion properties, social gathering sponsorship) is disproportionately effective vs. any other APAC market. Referral tracking in CRM is mandatory — when a new high-value client cites an existing Aurelle client as their introduction, both must be noted and the existing client recognized with a gratitude gesture.

INDIA

Revenue target FY2025: USD 22M (fastest-growing market, +35% projected YoY). Boutiques: Mumbai DLF Emporio equivalent (flagship, USD 12M), Delhi Select Citywalk equivalent (USD 6M), with Bangalore and Hyderabad boutiques opening Q2 2025 (USD 2M each planned).

India-Specific Context:
- Gold culture: India is the world's largest gold consumer. Aurelle's gold jewellery proposition (LOVE, Trinity, Panthère) aligns with deep cultural resonance — but the positioning must be elevated from 'gold jewelry' to 'heritage gold artistry' to justify price premium vs. local fine jewellers.
- BIS Hallmarking: All gold jewellery sold in India must carry BIS hallmark — this is handled at import/SDC level. Stylists must be able to explain hallmarking to clients as a quality guarantee, not a bureaucratic formality.
- Diamond education: Lab-grown diamond market is growing rapidly in India (35% of all diamond jewelry sold in FY2024 in India was lab-grown). Aurelle exclusively uses natural stones. Stylists must proactively explain the difference: rarity, investment, geological time, emotional legacy. Do not wait for the client to raise it.
- Wedding market: Indian wedding season (Oct–Dec, Feb–May) is the primary commercial driver. Bridal jewellery, men's watches as wedding gifts, and family gifting sets are peak demand. Bridal consultation protocol to be developed for Indian market specifically — typically involves multiple family members, multiple visits, longer decision cycle.
- High-net-worth profile: Tech sector wealth (Bangalore), traditional industrial wealth (Mumbai, Ahmedabad), new-generation entrepreneurs. Younger HNWI is more international, digital-first, interested in watch complications and international luxury status. Older HNWI gravitates toward gold jewellery weight and craftsmanship.

AUSTRALIA

Revenue target FY2025: USD 20M. Boutiques: Sydney Pitt Street Maison (flagship, USD 9M), Melbourne Collins Street (USD 6M), Brisbane (USD 3M), Perth (USD 2M). Australia presents a unique challenge in the Aurelle APAC portfolio: the market has the highest churn rate (24%), lowest emotional luxury attachment, and is most price-sensitive relative to income level among APAC markets. However, Australia is also home to a significant mining, finance, and tech wealth tier that represents a strong opportunity if
```

```
2025 Priority**: Singapore as APAC's regional events hub 
  for High Jewellery, anchored at ION Orchard Maison Mansion.\n\n
  
  ### India\n
  - **Priority**: Long-term market development. India is classified 
  as an 'Investment Market' in 2025 — boutique network is limited 
  to Delhi (DLF Emporio) and Mumbai (Bandra Kurla Complex). 
  Revenue targets are below APAC average but brand visibility 
  KPIs are elevated.\n
  - **Brand Narrative**: Wedding jewellery positioning is uniquely 
  powerful in India. Bespoke High Jewellery services and 
  bridal customization programs are the primary revenue driver.\n
  - **2025 Priority**: Introduce private client bridal consultation 
  program anchored in both boutiques. Expand CLV tracking 
  for NRI (Non-Resident Indian) clients shopping in Singapore, 
  HK, and Dubai.\n\n
  
  ### Australia & Taiwan\n
  - **Priority**: Steady-state premium market management. Both 
  markets demonstrate stable, repeat-driven client bases with 
  lower new-client acquisition velocity.\n
  - **Australia**: Sydney (Westfield Sydney) and Melbourne 
  (Collins Street) boutiques serve a high proportion of 
  Chinese-Australian affluent clientele. Communications 
  should be bilingual where CRM data supports.\n
  - **Taiwan**: Taipei 101 boutique serves as a high-visibility 
  flagship. Taiwan clients are among the most loyal in APAC 
  with above-average CLV tenure.\n\n
  
  ## Key Strategic Bets 2025\n
  1. **VIC Concentration Strategy**: Shift 15% of total marketing 
  budget from mass-digital to hyper-personalized VIC experiences 
  (private events, bespoke ateliers, Maison visits to Paris).\n
  2. **Watches Category Acceleration**: Capture growing 
  'investment watch' sentiment in Japan and Korea — position 
  Aurelle Complications as both cultural and financial assets.\n
  3. **Omnichannel Seamlessness**: By Q3 2025, all APAC markets 
  must offer a unified client profile viewable by stylists 
  in-boutique and CRM teams simultaneously, enabling 
  real-time personalization at point of sale.\n
  4. **Sustainability Narrative Integration**: 
  Aurelle's 'Responsible Sourcing Charter' (ethically sourced 
  diamonds, recycled precious metals) must be woven into all 
  High Jewellery and Fine Watchmaking client conversations — 
  mandatory from Q2 2025 per global Maison directive.\n\n
  
  ## Brand Governance & Compliance\n
  - All regional campaigns must receive validation from 
  Paris HQ Brand Direction team minimum 45 days pre-launch.\n
  - Local adaptations (language, talent casting, event formats) 
  are permitted within 'APAC Brand Expression Guidelines v3.2' 
  parameters.\n
  - Any deviation from Maison visual identity standards triggers 
  an automatic escalation to Regional VP Communications.
```

    *   If an entity or relationship was **missed** in the last task, extract and output it now according to the system format.

4.  **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
5.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.

---Role---
You are a Knowledge Graph Specialist responsible for extracting entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.
        *   `entity_description`: Provide a concise yet comprehensive description of the entity's attributes and activities, based *solely* on the information present in the input text.
    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

Based on the last extraction task, identify and extract any **missed or incorrectly formatted** entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Strict Adherence to System Format:** Strictly adhere to all format requirements for entity and relationship lists, including output order, field delimiters, and proper noun handling, as specified in the system instructions.
2.  **Focus on Corrections/Additions:**
    *   **Do NOT** re-output entities and relationships that were **correctly and fully** extracted in the last task.
    *   If an entity or relationship was **missed** in the last task, extract and output it now according to the system format.
    *   If an entity or relationship was **truncated, had missing fields, or was otherwise incorrectly formatted** in the last task, re-output the *corrected and complete* version in the specified format.
3.  **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
4.  **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
5.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.
6.  **Completion Signal:** Output `<|COMPLETE|>` as the final line after all relevant missing or corrected entities and relationships have been extracted and presented.
7.  **Output Language:** Ensure the output language is English. Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.

<Output>
---Role---
You are a Knowledge Graph Specialist responsible for extracting entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.
        *   `entity_description`: Provide a concise yet comprehensive description of the entity's attributes and activities, based *solely* on the information present in the input text.
    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

---Examples---
<Entity_types>
["Person","Creature","Organization","Location","Event","Concept","Method","Content","Data","Artifact","NaturalObject"]

```
while Alex clenched his jaw, the buzz of frustration dull against the backdrop of Taylor's authoritarian certainty. It was this competitive undercurrent that kept him alert, the sense that his and Jordan's shared commitment to discovery was an unspoken rebellion against Cruz's narrowing vision of control and order.

Then Taylor did something unexpected. They paused beside Jordan and, for a moment, observed the device with something akin to reverence. "If this tech can be understood..." Taylor said, their voice quieter, "It could change the game for us. For all of us."

The underlying dismissal earlier seemed to falter, replaced by a glimpse of reluctant respect for the gravity of what lay in their hands. Jordan looked up, and for a fleeting heartbeat, their eyes locked with Taylor's, a wordless clash of wills softening into an uneasy truce.

It was a small transformation, barely perceptible, but one that Alex noted with an inward nod. They had all been brought here by different paths
```

```
Document ID: DOC-006
Title: Aurelle Product Universe — Jewellery Portfolio APAC
Category: Product Knowledge
Content: Aurelle Jewellery Portfolio — APAC Sales & Product Knowledge Reference

PORTFOLIO OVERVIEW
Aurelle's jewellery portfolio is the Maison's founding purpose and deepest expression of identity. The portfolio spans four commercial tiers: iconic (LOVE, Trinity, Juste un Clou, Panthère motif), fine jewellery (Diamants Légers, Écrou de Aurelle, new seasonal collections), bridal & solitaires, and High Jewellery (unique or limited pieces in precious stones). Jewellery represents 48% of APAC revenue, with the iconic tier alone contributing 31% of total Aurelle APAC revenue — the single largest revenue cluster.

ICONIC COLLECTIONS — DETAILED PROFILES

LOVE COLLECTION
Heritage: Created in 1969 by Aldo Cipullo for the NYC clientele — a bracelet that could only be removed with a special screwdriver, a physical metaphor for love that cannot be undone. Worn by Elizabeth Taylor, Sophia Loren, and generations of devoted couples since.
Design DNA: Oval screw-motif bracelet in 18K gold (yellow, white, rose), set with screwheads as the only decoration. Available with and without diamonds. Extends to rings, earrings, pendants, and necklaces maintaining the screw-head motif.
APAC Commercial Role: Highest volume jewellery collection in APAC by units. The #1 gifting piece at Aurelle — Valentine's Day, anniversaries, Christmas, CNY. Also significant self-purchase by female clients who 'give themselves the LOVE.' The bracelet is a cultural signal in APAC: immediately recognized and aspirationally positioned.
Key References:
- LOVE Bracelet Yellow Gold (no stones): ~USD 6,750 — highest volume SKU in entire jewellery portfolio
- LOVE Bracelet Yellow Gold Full Diamonds: ~USD 21,500 — premium gifting, anniversaries
- LOVE Ring YG: ~USD 1,300 — highest volume entry, accessible aspirational
- LOVE Earrings (small screw): ~USD 2,100 — strong female self-purchase
- LOVE Necklace (small pendant): ~USD 1,850 — entry client acquisition
Gifting Selling Narrative: 'The LOVE bracelet cannot be removed without the screwdriver — a deliberate choice. It is not jewelry you wear sometimes. It is a commitment you wear always.'
Self-Purchase Narrative: 'The best love story you can tell starts with the love you have for yourself.'
Market Notes: In China, the LOVE bracelet is culturally linked to milestone gifting — 520 (Chinese Valentine's equivalent, May 20th) and 7/7 (Qixi Festival) generate 40% of annual LOVE bracelet revenue in 6 weeks. In Korea, LOVE ring is the #1 couple's ring at Aurelle. In Japan, the LOVE bangle (wider version) is gaining traction as a self-purchase luxury statement for working women. In Australia, LOVE is primarily Valentine's and anniversary — less self-purchase culture vs. Asia.
Cross-sell: LOVE bracelet YG → LOVE bracelet WG or RG as second or third unit (significant 'stacking' culture, especially in SG, HK, and CN). → Ballon Bleu watch as a pair. → High Jewellery once client relationship matures.

TRINITY COLLECTION
Heritage: Three interlocking bands of yellow, white, and rose gold, created in 1924. Jean Cocteau, Yves Saint Laurent, and countless cultural figures have worn it. The three golds traditionally represent fidelity, friendship, and love.
Design DNA: Three smooth bands, interlocked but free to move slightly — suggesting connection without constraint. The simplicity is deceptive; the tricolor execution requires extraordinary goldsmithing precision. Available as rings, bracelets, necklaces, earrings.
APAC Commercial Role: Second highest volume jewellery collection. Primary acquisition piece for Tier 3 clients — the first Aurelle jewellery many clients own. Entry price point makes it accessible; the heritage and recognizability make it prestigious. Equally popular male and female.
Key References:
- Trinity Ring Classic (3 bands, small): ~USD 980 — #1 Tier 3 acquisition piece
- Trinity Ring Large: ~USD 1,750 — upgrade from classic, strong in KR and CN
- Trinity Bracelet: ~USD 4,200 — step-up for Tier 2 clients
- Trinity Necklace: ~USD 3,100 — strong female self-purchase, Japan market
- Trinity Earrings: ~USD 2,400 — strong gifting
Selling Narrative: 'The three colors have always meant something different to every person who wears them — that ambiguity is intentional. Aurelle does not prescribe love; it celebrates its infinite forms.'
Market Notes: In Taiwan and Philippines, Trinity ring is the dominant entry piece — cultural preference for subtle, quality-first luxury over logo-driven pieces aligns with Trinity's understated design. In India, Trinity has grown 60% in FY2024 — the tricolor gold resonates with gold-appreciation culture while offering international modernity.
Cross-sell: Trinity Ring → LOVE bracelet (complementary size/weight balance). Trinity → Santos watch (shared geometric heritage — 'right angle meets triple circle' narrative). Trinity → Juste un Clou (styling contrast).

JUSTE UN CLOU
Heritage: Created 1971, also by Aldo Cipullo — a nail, bent into a bracelet. The ultimate triumph of conceptual art over conventional jewelry design. Initially considered provocative; now recognized as a landmark of 20th-century design.
Design DNA: A nail — the hardware store object — in 18K gold or platinum, set with diamonds. Available as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces. The tension between the mundane object and precious material creates the conversation.
APAC Commercial Role: High-margin
```

```
1971, also by Aldo Cipullo — a nail, bent into a bracelet. The ultimate triumph of conceptual art over conventional jewelry design. Initially considered provocative; now recognized as a landmark of 20th-century design.
Design DNA: A nail — the hardware store object — in 18K gold or platinum, set with diamonds. Available as bracelets, rings, earrings, and necklaces. The tension between the mundane object and precious material creates the conversation.
APAC Commercial Role: High-margin, strong with fashion-forward and culturally aware clients. Not the highest volume but commands above-average transaction values. Strong with creative industry clients (fashion, media, tech) who appreciate the conceptual backstory.
Key References:
- Juste un Clou Bracelet YG: ~USD 7,500
- Juste un Clou Ring WG Diamonds: ~USD 4,800
- Juste un Clou Earrings (nail motif): ~USD 3,200
Selling Narrative: 'When Cipullo bent a nail into gold, critics thought he was joking. Over fifty years later, it is still the most provocative bracelet Aurelle has ever made — because it asks: what is luxury if not a radical reimagining of the ordinary?'
Market Notes: Particularly strong in Tokyo (design culture), Seoul (fashion-forward young luxury), and Singapore (internationally educated creative class). Less intuitive in markets with stronger traditional jewelry preferences (India, some Mainland China tiers).

PANTHÈRE MOTIF JEWELLERY
Heritage: The panther has prowled through Aurelle's creative universe since 1914. Jeanne Toussaint — Aurelle's legendary creative director nicknamed 'La Panthère' — made it the Maison's totem animal.
Design DNA: Three-dimensional panther figures in white gold, yellow gold, and platinum, pavé-set with diamonds and black onyx spots, emerald eyes. Ranges from playful small pendants to fully sculptural High Jewellery brooches and necklaces.
APAC Commercial Role: Mid-to-high price point. Primarily Tier 1–2 clients. High emotional resonance — the panther is a statement about the wearer's character, not just their wealth. Strongest in Japan and Hong Kong.
Key References:
- Panthère de Aurelle Ring (onyx, emerald): ~USD 5,200
- Panthère Pendant Small WG Diamonds: ~USD 8,400
- Panthère Bracelet Full Pavé: ~USD 44,000 — Tier 1 territory
Selling Narrative: 'The panther has always been Aurelle's most personal symbol — not a heraldic crest, but a living character. Every woman who wears the Panthère chooses to embody what it represents: intelligence, grace, and the willingness to surprise.'

BRIDAL & SOLITAIRE
Aurelle's bridal offering ranges from classic solitaires to signature collections. The Destinée and 1895 collections are primary APAC bridal references.
- 1895 Solitaire (round brilliant, platinum): from USD 8,500 (0.5ct) to USD 85,000+ (3ct+)
- Destinée (innovative prong setting, diamond appears suspended): from USD 7,200
Market notes: Bridal is strongest in Japan (highest per-capita engagement ring spend globally, Aurelle competes directly with local and Tiffany), Taiwan, and South Korea. India's bridal segment is growing but requires GIA certification education — clients in Tier 1–2 increasingly want GIA alongside hallmarking. In China, lab-grown diamond awareness requires careful selling — Aurelle does not offer lab-grown diamonds; the natural stone heritage narrative must be proactively led.

FINE JEWELLERY COLLECTIONS (SEASONAL)
Seasonal collections (typically 2 per year) featuring fashion-forward designs, colored stones, and experimental forms. Examples: Coloratura (vibrant sapphire, emerald, rubies in geometric settings), Étourdissant de Aurelle (celestial motifs). These serve as conversation-starters for existing clients and trend-driven acquisition for fashion-oriented prospects. Average price range: USD 3,000–USD 25,000.
```

        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

```
Document ID: DOC-019
Title: Aurelle APAC Brand Strategy & Competitive Positioning 2025
Category: Strategy
Content: Aurelle APAC Brand Strategy & Competitive Positioning 2025\n\n
  
  ## Strategic Vision\n
  Aurelle's APAC brand strategy for 2025 operates under the global mandate 
  'Timeless Modernity' — preserving Maison heritage while accelerating 
  relevance across new-wealth demographics and digitally native luxury 
  consumers. APAC contributes 38% of Aurelle's global revenue, positioning 
  the region as the single most critical growth engine for the Maison 
  worldwide.\n\n
  
  ## Brand Positioning Framework\n
  Aurelle competes in the ultra-premium and high jewellery tier, 
  differentiated by three core pillars:\n
  1. **Savoir-Faire Authenticity**: All High Jewellery and Watchmaking 
  narratives anchor on Aurelle's 170-year Maison heritage, Parisian 
  ateliers, and hand-craftsmanship traditions. Regional communication 
  must never dilute this narrative.\n
  2. **Aspirational Accessibility**: Entry-level categories (Trinity 
  collection, Fragrances, and Santos de Aurelle entry watches) serve 
  as the client acquisition funnel, enabling aspirational consumers 
  to enter the Maison ecosystem before graduating to higher-value 
  categories.\n
  3. **Radical Exclusivity at the Top**: High Jewellery and 
  Ultra-High Complications remain deliberately scarce, reinforcing 
  perceived and intrinsic value at the Maison's apex.\n\n
  
  ## Competitive Landscape — APAC 2025\n
  Aurelle's primary competitive set in APAC comprises:\n
  - **Tier 1 Rivals** (direct competition, full category overlap): 
  Van Cleef & Arpels, Bvlgari, Chopard, Harry Winston.\n
  - **Tier 2 Rivals** (watches and accessories overlap): Rolex, 
  Patek Philippe, IWC, Jaeger-LeCoultre.\n
  - **Emerging Threat**: Chinese domestic luxury brands (Chow Tai 
  Fook Jewellery's high-end sub-brands, NE·TIGER) showing growing 
  traction in Tier 2 Chinese cities. These are monitored quarterly 
  by the Regional Strategy team but do not yet compete at the 
  High Jewellery level.\n\n
  Aurelle's differentiation advantage is the **Trinity-to-Haute-Joaillerie 
  continuum** — no direct competitor offers the same breadth of product 
  from entry-level gifting to USD 5M+ bespoke creations under a single 
  coherent Maison narrative.\n\n
  
  ## Market-Specific Brand Priorities\n
  
  ### Greater China (Mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau)\n
  - **Priority**: Volume leadership and VIC cultivation. China 
  remains the highest absolute revenue contributor within APAC.\n
  - **Brand Narrative**: Emphasize Maison heritage storytelling 
  through WeChat Channels, RED (Xiaohongshu), and Douyin short-form 
  content. Localize storytelling without altering brand codes.\n
  - **Key Risk**: Grey market price arbitrage remains a structural 
  threat, particularly for LOVE Bracelet and Santos segments. 
  Pricing harmonization protocols (see DOC-Finance) are critical.\n
  - **2025 Priority Cities**: Shanghai, Beijing, Chengdu, Shenzhen, 
  Hangzhou (digital activation only).\n\n
  
  ### Japan\n
  - **Priority**: Margin quality over volume. Japanese clientele 
  demonstrate the highest product knowledge and brand sensitivity 
  in APAC. Quality of service and heritage articulation are 
  non-negotiable.\n
  - **Brand Narrative**: Craftsmanship, timelessness, and 
  understated prestige resonate most strongly.\n
  - **2025 Priority**: Expand Watches category share — Santos de 
  Aurelle and Ballon Bleu — in response to resurgent local 
  watch demand and inbound tourist spending at Ginza boutique.\n\n
  
  ### South Korea\n
  - **Priority**: Younger affluent demographics (25–38 age bracket). 
  Korea leads APAC in influencer-driven luxury discovery; 
  Aurelle must balance exclusivity narrative with targeted 
  digital visibility.\n
  - **Brand Narrative**: Couple gifting rituals (LOVE, Trinity) 
  continue to anchor Korean market activations. High Jewellery 
  awareness is growing through K-celebrity ambassador alignment.\n
  - **2025 Priority**: Cheongdam boutique renovation completion 
  and hosting of first Seoul High Jewellery Gala event.\n\n
  
  ### Southeast Asia (Singapore, Thailand, Philippines)\n
  - **Priority**: VIC network deepening. SEA has a smaller but 
  highly concentrated ultra-high-net-worth client base. 
  Private salon events outperform boutique walk-in traffic 
  by a ratio of 3:1 in revenue terms.\n
  - **Brand Narrative**: Heritage + global prestige signaling. 
  SEA clients are highly travel-retail aware and compare 
  Paris/London/Dubai pricing. Consistent cross-market pricing 
  is critical.\n
  - **2025 Priority**: Singapore as APAC's regional events hub 
  for High Jewellery, anchored at ION Orchard Maison Mansion.\n\n
  
  ### India\n
  - **Priority**: Long-term market development. India is classified 
  as an 'Investment Market' in 2025 — boutique network is limited 
  to Delhi (DLF Emporio) and Mumbai (Bandra Kurla Complex). 
  Revenue targets are below APAC average but brand visibility 
  KP
```

---Instructions---

    *   **Do NOT** re-output entities and relationships that were **correctly and fully** extracted in the last task.
    *   If an entity or relationship was **missed** in the last task, extract and output it now according to the system format.
    *   If an entity or relationship was **truncated, had missing fields, or was otherwise incorrectly formatted** in the last task, re-output the *corrected and complete* version in the specified format.
3.  **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
4.  **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
5.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.
6.  **Completion Signal:** Output `<|COMPLETE|>` as the final line after all relevant missing or corrected entities and relationships have been extracted and presented.
7.  **Output Language:** Ensure the output language is English. Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.

<Output>
---Role---
You are a Knowledge Graph Specialist responsible for extracting entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.
        *   `entity_description`: Provide a concise yet comprehensive description of the entity's attributes and activities, based *solely* on the information present in the input text.
    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

---Examples---

```
Document ID: DOC-005
Title: Aurelle Product Universe — Watches Portfolio APAC
Category: Product Knowledge
Content: Aurelle Watches Portfolio — APAC Sales & Product Knowledge Reference

PORTFOLIO OVERVIEW
Aurelle's watch collections represent 38% of APAC revenue and serve as the primary acquisition vehicle for clients under 40. The portfolio spans from entry luxury (Tank Must, ~USD 3,200) to Grand Complications (Rotonde Ultra-Thin Perpetual Calendar, ~USD 98,000+), offering a complete journey from first purchase to ultimate horological expression. Aurelle occupies the position of the 'architect's watch' — design-led, geometrically distinctive, heritage-rich but never anachronistic.

COLLECTION PROFILES

SANTOS DE AURELLE
Heritage: Designed 1904 for Alberto Santos-Dumont, the aviation pioneer — the first wristwatch made for a man of action. One of the most copied designs in history, which reinforces original ownership narrative.
Design DNA: Square case with exposed screws on bezel and bracelet — the signature 'vis' detail. Available in steel, SSGP (Aurelle's proprietary steel-gold blend), bicolor, and full gold. Interchangeable strap system (Quiklage) is a key client engagement feature — stylists should demonstrate in boutique.
APAC Commercial Role: Top revenue-generating watch collection. Primary target: male clients 28–45, professional sector, first serious watch purchase or step-up from fashion watches. Strongest markets: China (aspirational professional), Korea (style-driven), Australia (entry luxury male demographic).
Key References:
- Santos Medium Steel: ~USD 7,100 — highest volume SKU in APAC
- Santos Large SSGP: ~USD 8,500 — most popular bicolor, strong in KR and CN
- Santos de Aurelle Skeleton: ~USD 19,500 — high visibility piece, boutique display anchor
- Santos 100 Chronograph: ~USD 14,200 — motorsport/aviation client appeal
Selling Narrative: 'The Santos was the world's first functional wristwatch — not made for fashion, but for flight. Every Santos client today inherits that spirit of purposeful innovation.'
Cross-sell: After Santos acquisition, highest cross-sell success to LOVE bracelet (gifting for partner) or second Santos in different material.

TANK MUST
Heritage: The Tank was born in 1917, inspired by the aerial view of military tanks on the Western Front. Has been worn by every icon from Jackie Kennedy to Andy Warhol to Princess Diana.
Design DNA: Pure rectangular geometry, Roman numeral dial, cabochon crown — the most copied watch silhouette in history. Tank Must is the heritage-accessible entry: quartz movement, lacquered dials in color options, SolarBeat™ photovoltaic movement (sustainable positioning).
APAC Commercial Role: Highest unit volume watch in APAC. Primary target: female clients 25–45, second watch (complement to sports/casual watch already owned), gift purchase. Also strong with male clients seeking a dress watch.
Key References:
- Tank Must Small Quartz (lacquered colors): ~USD 3,200 — entry luxury, highest gifting volume
- Tank Must Large SolarBeat™: ~USD 4,100 — sustainability messaging resonates in AU, SG, JP
- Tank Louis (higher heritage tier): ~USD 7,800 — for clients seeking the 'pure' Tank expression
- Tank Cintrée (limited, elongated vintage form): ~USD 12,500 — highest desirability, waitlist in JP and TW
Selling Narrative: 'The Tank has been on the wrists of presidents, artists, and icons for over a century — not because of what it says about wealth, but because of what it says about taste.'
Cross-sell: Tank → Trinity ring (shared geometric heritage narrative). Tank → La Panthère watch (for clients seeking something more expressive).

BALLON BLEU DE AURELLE
Heritage: Launched 2007 — Aurelle's most commercially successful modern creation. Named for the blue sapphire cabochon protecting the winding crown, an innovation that became a new luxury signifier.
Design DNA: Round case with softly curved lugs, signature blue cabochon crown concealed in the case arch — feminine yet substantial. Available in 33mm, 36mm, and 42mm — true unisex range. Steel, bicolor, full gold, and diamond-set versions.
APAC Commercial Role: Female-dominant purchase (72% female buyers APAC). Strongest emotional connection of any watch in the portfolio. Highest cross-category collector depth: clients who buy Ballon Bleu often also purchase La Panthère or High Jewellery.
Key References:
- Ballon Bleu 33mm Steel: ~USD 6,800 — entry, highest female volume
- Ballon Bleu 36mm Bicolor Diamond Bezel: ~USD 11,500 — highest revenue per unit in collection
- Ballon Bleu 42mm Full Gold: ~USD 28,000 — male buyer, wealth signal
- Ballon Bleu Tourbillon: ~USD 72,000 — top of range, HJ client crossover
Selling Narrative: 'The blue cabochon is not a stone chosen for decoration — it is a functional guardian of the movement, a touch of poetry in a world of precision.'
Cross-sell: Ballon Bleu 33mm → Ballon Bleu 36mm upgrade within 24 months (highest same-collection repeat purchase rate: 34%). Also cross-sells strongly to LOVE bracelet and Trinity.

LA PANTHÈRE DE AURELLE
Heritage: The Panthère emblem has been Aurelle's most iconic motif since the 1910s
```

---Task---
Extract entities and relationships from the input text in Data to be Processed below.

---Instructions---
1.  **Strict Adherence to Format:** Strictly adhere to all format requirements for entity and relationship lists, including output order, field delimiters, and proper noun handling, as specified in the system prompt.
2.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.
3.  **Completion Signal:** Output `<|COMPLETE|>` as the final line after all relevant entities and relationships have been extracted and presented.
4.  **Output Language:** Ensure the output language is English. Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.

---Data to be Processed---
<Entity_types>
[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]

<Input Text>
```
Document ID: DOC-008
Title: Market Intelligence — Greater China (Mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau)
Category: Market Intelligence
Content: Greater China Market Intelligence Report — FY2025 Outlook

MARKET OVERVIEW
Greater China remains Aurelle's largest single market cluster, representing USD 310M of APAC revenue target (50% of regional total). The three sub-markets operate in complementary dynamics: Mainland China drives volume and new client acquisition; Hong Kong serves as a sophisticated cross-border hub; Macau captures tourism-linked luxury spend from Mainland VIPs.

MAINLAND CHINA

Economic Context (FY2025):
Post-COVID recovery has been uneven — high-end luxury (>USD 10,000 transaction value) has proven resilient, while aspirational luxury (USD 1,000–5,000) faces pressure from domestic consumption confidence softness. Aurelle's positioning in the mid-to-high tier is structurally advantageous. GDP growth projected at 4.8%, luxury sector outperforming at +8–10% for established international maisons. Government anti-corruption regulation remains a background consideration — corporate gifting in institutional channels is constrained, but private HNW gifting continues strongly.

Consumer Profile:
- Primary buyer demographic: Female, 28–40, Tier 1–2 cities, professional or entrepreneurial
- Rising segment: Male HNWI 30–45, watches dominant purchase, jewellery gifting
- Second-generation wealth ('Fuerdai'): Cultural consumers, internationally educated, seek brand depth not just logos — Heritage narrative critical
- Geographic wealth shift: Chengdu, Hangzhou, Nanjing, Shenzhen increasingly important — not just Beijing/Shanghai

Digital Ecosystem (Critical):
- WeChat is the operating system of luxury in China: WeChat Mini-Program handles discovery, appointment booking, wish-list sharing (clients share wish-lists with gifting partners via WeChat), and post-purchase service
- RED (Xiaohongshu): #1 luxury aspiration platform — Aurelle content strategy on RED is managed by China Marketing team; stylists may reshare approved content
- Weibo: Declining relevance for luxury, maintained for KOL relationships
- Douyin (TikTok China): Growing for brand awareness among 18–28 demographic; not yet a sales-conversion platform for Aurelle
- KOL (Key Opinion Leader) Strategy: Aurelle China maintains relationships with 12 Tier-1 KOLs (celebrity-level, >5M followers) and 45 KOCs (Key Opinion Consumers — authentic HNW individuals sharing genuine purchases). KOC strategy is more valuable for conversion than KOL for awareness

Key Dates (China Market):
- January–February: CNY preparation — highest gifting season, 30–35% of H1 revenue
- May 20 (520 Day): Couples gifting — LOVE bracelet peak, equivalent in commercial weight to Valentine's Day
- June 18 (618 Shopping Festival): Digital-led promotions; Aurelle does not discount but activates exclusive digital content and limited online CRM events
- October (Golden Week): Tourism recovery, both domestic travel to boutiques and Mainland visitors to HK/Macau
- November 11 (Singles' Day): Luxury brands have progressively withdrawn from deep discount participation; Aurelle uses this period for digital brand content only
- December: Christmas and New Year gifting, watch and jewellery balanced

Competition in China:
- Bulgari: Aggressive boutique expansion, strong in new wealth tier with Serpenti positioning
- Tiffany (LVMH): Repositioned as higher-tier post-acquisition, direct competition for LOVE-equivalent (T bracelet) client
- Van Cleef: Strongest competitor in High Jewellery and fine jewellery; Alhambra motif has achieved cultural saturation similar to LOVE in some demographics
- Domestic brands (Lao Feng Xiang, Chow Tai Fook): Compete for aspirational tier but are not direct luxury competitors for Aurelle's core positioning
- Key differentiator: Aurelle's dual identity (watches + jewellery under one Maison roof) is a genuine competitive moat — no competitor matches both categories at equivalent depth

HONG KONG

Market Role: Hong Kong serves as Aurelle's global benchmark for retail excellence — highest average transaction value boutique globally (Landmark flagship). It is simultaneously: (1) a domestic luxury market for HK residents, (2) a Mainland tourist destination for cross-border shoppers, (3) a hub for international clients (Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, Western expats).
Revenue Target FY2025: USD 78M (included in Greater China total)
Key Dynamics:
- Landmark Hong Kong Maison: 1,800 sqm, highest per-sqm productivity in APAC. Renovation completed 2024 with expanded HJ salon.
- Cross-border shopping recovery: Mainland visits to HK luxury boutiques recovering to approximately 65% of 2019 levels (FY2025 projection). Simplified CRM data-sharing across CN/HK sub-markets improves client recognition.
- Local HK client: Sophisticated, internationally traveled, watches-informed (proximity to independent watch retail culture). Appreciates technical detail and understated presentation.
- Price differential: With current currency dynamics, HK pricing is approximately 8–12% below equivalent European purchase for Chinese tourists — a commercial argument that must be handled carefully (price shopping diminishes relationship quality; stylists should refocus conversation to relationship and experience value).

MACAU

Market Role: Casino resort ecosystem drives a unique client profile — mainland VHNWIs visiting for entertainment, often in groups, with significant discretionary spend in a compressed timeframe.
Revenue Target FY2025: USD 22M
Key Dynamics:
- Aurelle Macau operates 2 boutiques: Wynn Palace (highest performance) and Galaxy Macau
- Client profile: Very high CLV potential but low retention rate — many clients do not return. CRM capture rate is a critical metric (target: capture full
```

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.

    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

8.  **Completion Signal:** Output the literal string `<|COMPLETE|>` only after all entities and relationships, following all criteria, have been completely extracted and outputted.

```
decoration — it is a functional guardian of the movement, a touch of poetry in a world of precision.'
Cross-sell: Ballon Bleu 33mm → Ballon Bleu 36mm upgrade within 24 months (highest same-collection repeat purchase rate: 34%). Also cross-sells strongly to LOVE bracelet and Trinity.

LA PANTHÈRE DE AURELLE
Heritage: The Panthère emblem has been Aurelle's most iconic motif since the 1910s — adopted as the totem of the Maison, personifying elegance, power, and sensuality.
Design DNA: Square-in-round case (the 'panther eye' aperture), hidden in a bracelet that IS the watch — blurring jewelry and timepiece. Full gold or bicolor with onyx and diamond pavé details.
APAC Commercial Role: Highest average transaction value watch after complications. Target: female clients, Tier 1–2, fashion and jewelry-forward. Strong in Japan (aesthetic refinement culture), Korea (brand status), and HK.
Key References:
- Panthère Small Steel: ~USD 5,500 — entry jewelry-watch
- Panthère Medium Full Gold Diamond Bracelet: ~USD 34,000 — top Tier 2 / Tier 1 cross-sell
- Panthère Tourbillon: ~USD 115,000 — High Jewellery territory, allocated individually
Selling Narrative: 'The Panthère does not tell time — it commands it. Every client who wears it joins a century of Aurelle women who never followed, only led.'

ROTONDE DE AURELLE — COMPLICATIONS
Heritage: Aurelle's horological pinnacle — showcasing the Manufacture's mastery of grand complications including tourbillon, perpetual calendar, minute repeater, and celestial charts.
APAC Commercial Role: Low volume, very high value. Primary audience: serious watch collectors, Tier 1 Icon clients. Japan is by far the strongest market (collector culture). Also growing in China among second-generation wealth inheritors with horological interest.
Key References:
- Rotonde Ultra-Thin Perpetual Calendar: ~USD 98,000
- Rotonde Tourbillon: ~USD 165,000
- Rotonde Minute Repeater Flying Tourbillon: ~USD 410,000 — APAC allocation: 4 pieces annually
Selling Notes: Requires specialized Watch Specialist (not standard stylist) to present. Client knowledge should be gauged before technical depth — some clients are experts, others are buying the emotional narrative of rarity. Do not over-explain to the former; do not under-explain to the latter.

ULYSSE — SPORT & CONTEMPORARY (NEW FY2025)
New collection launching Q2 2025. Aurelle's answer to the sport-luxury segment (competing with AP Royal Oak adjacency in positioning). Target: male clients 30–45, HNWI with active lifestyle. Steel and titanium. Integrated bracelet. Swiss manufacture automatic movement.
Initial references: 40mm and 44mm. Pricing: USD 12,500–USD 19,000.
Strategic rationale: Recapture clients currently choosing sport-luxury alternatives while staying in Aurelle relationship. Pre-launch waitlist to be managed via CRM — priority to Tier 1 Santos and Ballon Bleu collectors.

GENERAL WATCH SELLING PRINCIPLES FOR APAC
1. Always begin with the heritage story — price follows meaning, not the reverse
2. Demonstrate the physical object: Quiklage strap change for Santos, sapphire cabochon for Ballon Bleu, the bracelet transformation for Panthère
3. Market-specific sensitivity: Japan clients value movement quality and restraint; Korean clients value recognizability and lifestyle imagery; Chinese clients value heritage narrative and investment narrative; Australian clients value practicality and design story
4. Never position Aurelle watches against competitors directly — elevate to Aurelle's own universe
5. The 'watch of a lifetime' narrative is valid for complications; the 'signature of style' narrative for Tank and Panthère; the 'tool for ambition' narrative for Santos
```
